failure_modes.text: Teams can treat market behavior as fully rational and miss the emotional or political forces shaping demand.
failure_modes.mitigation: Include behavioral and political forces in the demand model: check whether emotional, cultural, or regulatory dynamics are shaping willingness to pay beyond rational utility calculations.

failure_modes.text: A simple price-volume frame can torture reality when the real problem is novel or behaviorally distorted.
failure_modes.source_quote: Torturing Reality to Fit the Model
failure_modes.mitigation: Test frame fit before applying a standard price-volume model: verify that the market behaves like a textbook supply-demand system rather than force-fitting the frame onto novel dynamics.

failure_modes.text: Accurate economic reasoning can still be useless if it does not convert into actionable frontline choices.
failure_modes.source_quote: An accurate but useless idea is still useless
failure_modes.mitigation: Translate economic insight into frontline actions: convert supply-demand analysis into specific pricing, inventory, or messaging decisions that operators can execute.

premortem_questions.text: Which non-economic forces are distorting demand or constraining supply in this case?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What non-economic principles might be influencing customer decisions

premortem_questions.text: What second-order consequences follow from this price or capacity move?
Is our core message simple enough that people can act on it, not just agree with it?

heuristics.text: Use demand segmentation to identify which motives and market gaps actually matter.
Align product benefits with the beliefs, desires, and pains of the buyer.
Model price, volume, and market share together instead of changing one variable in isolation.
